title: A renormalization-group inspired lattice-based framework for piecewise generalized linear models
abstract: We formally introduce a class of models inspired by renormalization group (RG) theory, built on additive hierarchical expansions analogous to those appearing in functional ANOVA and mixed-effects models. Like ReLU convolutional neural networks, they are almost everywhere locally linear; unlike ReLU networks, their partition structure is explicit, interpretable, and easy to modify or constrain. In these models, one defines a multidimensional lattice partition of the input space and uses it to scaffold variations in regression parameters. Each dimension of the lattice corresponds to an attribute by which the statistics of the problem may vary. The parameters are themselves expressed in the form of an expansion, where each term captures variations relative to a lower (coarser) interaction scale. These models admit multiple equivalent interpretations: as piecewise GLMs, as hierarchical mixed-effects regressions, or as regression trees with structured parameter sharing. Since RG motivates the design of these models, we use techniques from statistical physics -- specifically replica analysis -- to study their generalization properties. Specifically, we analyze the behavior of the Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion (WAIC) as a proxy for generalization loss. This analysis yields two practical results: (i) guidance on the lattice design as a function of dataset size and predictor dimensionality; and (ii) a principled scaling law for the regularization prior when adding higher-order terms to the expansion so that one can increase model complexity without an expected increase in generalization loss. We evaluate the methodology on public datasets and find performance competitive against both blackbox methods and other intrinsically interpretable approaches.
summary: The paper introduces a new class of interpretable models based on a lattice-structured expansion of parameters. By applying Renormalization Group theory, the authors derive a scaling law for regularization that allows the model to capture complex interactions without overfitting. The method performs competitively against standard machine learning algorithms on several benchmark datasets.
main_problem: Developing an intrinsically interpretable, piecewise GLM framework that allows for increasing model complexity (higher-order interactions) without the typical bias-variance penalty or overfitting.
main_result: The derivation of a 'generalization-preserving' scaling law for regularization allows for adding interaction terms without increasing expected generalization loss, achieving performance competitive with black-box models.
method: The framework uses Renormalization Group (RG) theory and replica analysis to derive scaling laws for priors, treating parameters as thermal variables and data as quenched disorder.
model_or_system: A multidimensional lattice-based piecewise GLM where parameters are decomposed into a hierarchical expansion of global, mesoscopic, and local interaction components.
key_observables: Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion (WAIC), generalization gap (delta S_K), effective degrees of freedom (d_eff), and shrinkage factor (s).
important_parameters: Lattice resolution (L), interaction order (K), decay rate of effect sizes (rho), parameter-to-sample ratio (gamma), and prior standard deviation (tau).
main_assumptions: Assumes replica symmetry in the posterior, uses Laplace approximation for the WAIC variance, and focuses on the typical-case generalization properties via the replica method.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows hierarchical parameter decomposition across scales; Figure 2 demonstrates the superiority of the theory-based regularization scheme; Figure 3 validates the RG flow via Test MSE and SNR.
paper_structure: Introduction of the lattice-based GLM framework; theoretical derivation of scaling laws using RG and replica analysis; implementation details including optimization and discretization; experimental validation on various UCI datasets; discussion of limitations and future work.
why_interesting: The use of replica analysis and Renormalization Group theory to study the generalization properties of a neural-network-like architecture provides a direct bridge between statistical physics of disordered systems and machine learning.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2: Generalization-preserving regularization validation. Improvement in test log-likelihood per observation relative to unregularized baseline across truncation orders K ∈{0, 1, 2}. Positive values indicate better generalization. The theory-based scheme (blue circles) achieves 147–204 LL units improvement; fixed and ad-hoc decay (gray) show no improvement.

Figure 3: RG flow verification using fitted model parameters. Left: Test MSE (lower is better) decreases with truncation order; K = 2 achieves optimal generalization. Center: SNR by interaction order; pairwise effects (order 2) have higher SNR than main effects. Right: Generalization gap ∆SK (negative is good) confirms each order up to K = 2 improves generalization.
